items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.

items.q: When might an autocratic leadership style be appropriate?
items.correct: In crises requiring quick decisions, when workers are inexperienced, or in highly hierarchical contexts — though it can damage morale long-term.
items.misconception: Autocratic leadership is always wrong and democratic leadership is always right.
items.random_wrong: Autocratic leaders are dictators who break the law.
items.why: Students moralise leadership styles. The business studies view is contingency-based — each style has appropriate contexts. Democratic is not universally best; autocracy works in emergencies.

items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Specific. “Strategic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: Correct answer: Friedman. “Drucker” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 elements. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
